coredata.dc:title: Preparation of blue-color-emitting graphene quantum dots and their in vitro and in vivo toxicity evaluation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Nafiujjaman, Md
Kim, Jeongho
Park, Hun-Kuk
Lee, Yong-Kyu
coredata.dc:description: We synthesized and characterized blue-color-emitting graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) which is better than that of other GQDs synthesized from organic molecules. We investigated the biocompatibility of BGQDs with cells and erythrocytes. Evaluation of epithelial carcinoma cancer cells indicated that most cells successfully took up BGQDs and toxicity testing showed no effect on cell viability. We also examined alterations in deformability and aggregation of human erythrocytes exposed to BGQDs at different concentrations and incubation times. These results suggest that BGQDs are biocompatible and do not cause cell membrane damage, and thus, are promising nanomaterials for various medical diagnoses and treatments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Quantum yield
Cytotoxicity
Erythrocyte
Hemorheology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of blue-color-emitting graphene quantum dot (BGQD) synthesis. (b) Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) image shows morphology of BGQDs in aqueous solution at 100μg/mL (scale bar 100nm). Hydrodynamic size of BGQDs (inset). (c) Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image showing morphology and height of BGQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows morphology of BGQDs in aqueous solution at 100μg/mL (scale bar 100nm). (a, inset) A typical single BGQD with the lattice parameter of 0.234nm. (b) Fast Fourier transform (FFT) image of BGQDs.

(a) Ultraviolet (UV) and (b) fluorescence intensity (FI) spectra of BGQDs at 1000μg/mL in aqueous solution. (c) Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) image of BGQDs and (d) table showing percentage (%) of elements.

(a) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of BGQDs at 1mg/mL. (b) Concentration-based emission spectra of BGQDs (c) pH-based emission spectra of BGQDs at 0.5mg/mL. (d) Stability test by fluorescence spectra of BGQDs in an aqueous solution for 4days at 1.5mg/mL at pH 7.

(a) Cell uptake studies of BGQDs. Fluorescence images of KB cells incubated with saline (control) or 250μg/mL BGQDs for 4h at 37°C. (b) Intracellular distribution of BGQDs shown by co-localization with cell organelle-specific dyes (propidium iodide for nuclear staining). Fluorescence images of PI co-stained KB cells incubated with 250μg/mL of BGQDs for 4h at 37°C.

Cell viability of BGQDs at different concentrations (50–1000μg/mL) in KB (a) and MDCK cells (b) incubated for 24h at 37°C. (c) Fluorescence images of calcein AM and propidium iodide (PI) co-staining of KB cells incubated with saline (control), 1000μg/mL of BGQDs for 24h at 37°C.
In vivo bioimaging of BGQDs injected to mice at different time points (0–24h). One hundred microliters of 5mg/mL/kg of BGQDs were injected intravenously via the tail vein into mice.

(a) Images of hemolysis due to hemoglobin released from RBCs after 2-h exposure to BGQDs at different concentrations. (b) Percent hemolysis of RBCs incubated with different concentrations of BGQDs. Absorbance of red hemoglobin in the supernatant was measured at 540nm after 2h. Mean values±SD are given, n=3. * denotes p<0.05.

(a) RBC images obtained from a diffraction pattern after 3-h exposure to BGQDs at the following concentrations: 0μg/mL (control), 500μg/mL, 750μg/mL, and 1000μg/mL, respectively. (b) Elongation index of RBCs after exposure to BGQDs for 3h. (c) Elongation index of RBCs for different incubation times (0, 1, 2, 3h). Mean values±SD are given, n=3.

(a) Changes in aggregation of RBCs after 3h exposure of BGQDs at the concentration of 0, 500, 750, and 1000μg/mL, respectively. (b) Changes in aggregation of RBCs exposed to BGQDs for different incubation times (0, 1, 2, 3h). Mean values±SD are shown, n=3.

Field emission scanning electron microscope images of RBCs exposed to different concentrations of BGQDs for 3h. (a) Control (b) 500μg/mL (c) 750μg/mL (d) 1000μg/mL. SEM images were taken at ×1000, ×5000, ×10,000, and ×20,000 magnification. Scale bars represent 50, 10, 5, and 2μm, respectively.

head.ce:title: Preparation of blue-color-emitting graphene quantum dots and their in vitro and in vivo toxicity evaluation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Green Bioengineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chunbuk, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Medicine, Kyung Hee University, Seoul, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical & Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chunbuk, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Biomedical Engineering, College of Medicine, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea.
Corresponding author at: Department of Green Bioengineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Daehak-ro, Chungju-si, Chunbuk 27469, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We synthesized and characterized blue-color-emitting graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) which is better than that of other GQDs synthesized from organic molecules. We investigated the biocompatibility of BGQDs with cells and erythrocytes. Evaluation of epithelial carcinoma cancer cells indicated that most cells successfully took up BGQDs and toxicity testing showed no effect on cell viability. We also examined alterations in deformability and aggregation of human erythrocytes exposed to BGQDs at different concentrations and incubation times. These results suggest that BGQDs are biocompatible and do not cause cell membrane damage, and thus, are promising nanomaterials for various medical diagnoses and treatments.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene nanomaterials and its derivatives have attracted tremendous attention in various research areas because of their optical, electrochemical, and biocompatible properties [1]. In addition, they have unique properties such as blue to green luminescence, excellent photostability, intrinsic physical and chemical characteristics, and biocompatibility [2]. There are few bioimaging and optoelectronic applications for graphene materials because of their zero bandgap properties [3]. As a result, photoluminescent graphene, which generates a bandgap, has been developed.

There are various approaches for synthesizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which can typically be divided into two groups: top–down and bottom up methods [4]. Top–down methods are based on exfoliation of carbon materials to form small sizes [5]. Photoluminescent GQDs can be obtained from carbon sources by a top–down method, where reaction conditions and purification processes are complicated and time-consuming. This top down method is used several steps, making it very difficult purify additional products. This method also uses acid solutions. Additionally, the top–down method produced graphene with low product yield and low quantum yield [6]. In contrast to top–down approaches, bottom-up methods fabricate GQDs from small carbon precursors [7] in a one-step process [8]. However, bottom-up methods have some limitations such as complex purification processes and their time-consuming nature.

Because GQDs are chemically inert compared to conventional quantum dots, they are not toxic to cells [9]. However, the toxicity mechanism of GQDs in a wide variety of biomedical applications requires further investigation. Yuan et al. [10] reported that the cytotoxicity of different functionalized groups on modified GQDs in human lung carcinoma (A549) and human C6 glioma cells at a concentration of 200μg/mL. Nurunnabi et al. [11] found no in vitro and in vivo toxicity of carboxylated GQDs at concentrations of 500μg/mL and 10mg/kg, respectively. Jiang et al. [12] demonstrated that low-dose GQDs had little effect on zebrafish embryos and larvae.

Most in vivo and in vitro studies investigated the hemolysis and morphology of red blood cells (RBCs) to evaluate nanoparticle toxicity [13–15]. A few papers have reported the effects of nanomaterial size and aspect ratio on hemorheological changes in human RBCs such as deformability and aggregation [16,17]. Some studies of graphene toxicity have investigated the hemolysis and alteration of RBC morphology, but the effects on deformability and aggregation of RBCs remain unknown. GQDs are less hemolytic than other nanomaterials are at higher concentrations [13,18–20]. Their results demonstrated that GQD-treated RBCs showed significant morphological alterations such as echinocytic or acanthocytic shapes.

The purpose of this study was to synthesize blue-color emitting GQDs (BGQDs) with high quantum yield using a simple and rapid bottom-up method by carbonizing l-glutamic acid, based on a previous method with minor modifications [6,21]. We evaluated in vitro toxicity on normal cells (MDCK) and cancer cells (KB) as well as in vivo in mice. We also investigated the hemolysis, deformability, and aggregation of RBCs exposed to BGQDs and observed RBC shapes by scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

BGQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing l-glutamic acid as previously reported with minor modifications [6,21]. Briefly, 2g l-glutamic acid was added to a glass bottle and heated to 220°C using a heating mantle. The solid l-glutamic acid changed to liquid, and the boiling colorless liquid turned brown, indicating the formation of BGQDs. Next, 10mL water was added to the solution followed by stirring for 30min. The solution was cooled to room temperature, centrifuged at 10,000× g for 30min, and the supernatant was collected.

The synthesized BGQDs were characterized by ultraviolet (UV) spectroscopy (Optizen 2120UV; Mecasys Co., Ltd., Daejeon, Korea) to analyze the absorbance of BGQDs. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (FS-2; SCINCO, Seoul, Korea) was performed to evaluate the emission profiles of BGQDs. The morphology and height of BGQDs were characterized using an atomic force microscope (AFM) (Nanoscope IV; Veeco Instruments Inc., Plainview, NY, USA) operating in tapping mode. BGQD morphology was observed using a JSM-7610F field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM; JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The average diameter of BGQDs in distilled water was measured by Dynamic light scattering (DLS) at a fixed scatting angle of 90° at 25°C using a Zetasizer Nano S90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Worcestershire, UK) equipped with a 633nm He–Ne gas laser. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a SDT 2960 (TA Instruments, Inc., New Castle, DE, USA). The morphology of BGQDs was observed using a JEM-2100F field-emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL USA, Inc., Peabody, MA, USA). An energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS 51-XM X1034; Oxford Instruments, Inc., Abingdon, UK) attached to the FE-SEM with an operating voltage of 5kV, was used to analyze the relative carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen content of the BGQDs. The quantum yield of BGQDs was calculated by measuring the integrated PL intensity in aqueous solution against Rhodamine B with a quantum yield 31% [22].

The cellular uptake of BGQDs was evaluated in epithelial carcinoma (KB) cells and Madin Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) cells using a confocal laser scanning microscope (LSMS 10; Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). Lyophilized BGQDs were dispersed in fresh Roswell Park Memorial Institute-1640 medium at a concentration of 250μg/mL. The cells were incubated with the BGQD solution for 4h, washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and then fixed with 4% formaldehyde in PBS. All procedures were performed in the dark. Cell images were collected by confocal laser scanning microscopy paired with a long pass emission filter (488–543nm) and the BGQDs inside the cells were visualized [23].

KB and MDCK cells were seeded onto a 96-well plate (5×104 cells/well), incubated for 24h, and then treated with different concentrations of BGQDs using PBS as the control. The cells were incubated for another 24h in the dark at 37°C, and then 50μL of aqueous MTT solution (Sigma–Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) was added to each well of the 96-well plate 4h before terminating the 24-h incubation. The cells were incubated for another 4h, the upper layer of the solution was discarded, and then MTT solubilization solution (100μL) was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals by stirring with a pipette. Next, optical absorbance of the resulting solution was measured using a Varioskan Flash (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) at a wavelength of 570nm, and converted to cell viability by constructing a standard curve (absorbance vs. cell numbers) [24]. Cell viability was then calculated using the following equation:
(1)
Cell viability
( % ) =
ABS
sam
ABS cont
× 100
where ABSsam and ABScont are the absorbance values of the test and control samples, respectively.

The live/dead viability/cytotoxicity assay kit containing calcein acetoxymethyl ester (AM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and propidium iodide (Sigma–Aldrich) induced the live and dead cells to produce green and red emission, respectively; this was observed using fluorescence microscopy. Calcein AM shows green fluorescence after AM is hydrolyzed by intracellular esterases in live cells. Propidium iodide penetrates the nuclei of dead cells once the integrity of their cell membranes is compromised. KB cells (1×104 cells/well) were grown on 8-well plates for 24h, the medium was replaced with fresh cell culture medium, and the cells were treated with BGQDs and PBS. Cells were further incubated for 24h and then treated with the live/dead viability/cytotoxicity kit reagents before acquiring fluorescent microscopic images using an oil immersion 20× objective lens in two channels (488 and 543nm excitation for calcein AM and propidium iodide, respectively). This processed allowed for concurrent comparison of cells exposed to light and those without light exposure [25].

The 7–8-week-old nude mouse model was purchased from Orient Bio, Inc. (Gyeonggi, Korea) and were maintained under specific pathogen-free conditions. All experiments were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the Korea National University of Transportation in accordance with NIH guidelines [26]. Subcutaneous tumors were implanted by inoculating epithelial carcinoma (KB) cells into one side of the mouse body. When the tumor volume was approximately 100mm3, 100μL of 5mg/kg of BGQDs were injected intravenously via the tail vein into mice. Mice were then anesthetized with ketamine via intraperitoneal injection and images of BGQD-injected mice were taken by a time domain diffuse optical tomography system. All images were acquired using a 4000 MM Pro digital imaging system (Kodak, Rochester, NY, USA). Exposure time was 30s, and excitation and emission filters were 430 and 530nm, respectively [26].

Human blood from healthy male donors who had not taken any medicine during the previous week was collected using a 21G butterfly infusion and placed into a Vacutainer® (BD Biosciences, Franklin Lakes, NJ, USA) containing K2-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid as the anticoagulant. The Kyung Hee University Hospital Committee approved this study and the use of human blood (KMC IRB No: 161604). All experimental guidelines for human subject protocol review and biohazard control were followed [27]. Erythrocytes were separated from the plasma and buffy coat by centrifugation at 100× g for 15min. The remaining packed RBCs were washed twice with PBS and centrifuged at 300× g for 5min. Next, 500μL of the packed RBCs was diluted with 1.5mL PBS to obtain a suspension with a hematocrit of 25%. Next, 0.3mL of the RBC suspension was mixed with 1.2mL of the GQD dispersions in PBS at different concentrations (0, 500, 750, 1000, and 1250μg/mL) to obtain a final hematocrit of 5%. Additionally, 0.3mL of packed RBCs were added to 1.2mL PBS as a control sample. All final samples were placed on a roller mixer in an incubator at 37°C for 3h.

Red hemoglobin released from human RBCs exposed to different concentrations of BGQDs was measured as an indicator of hemolytic activity. After 2h of incubation, the samples were centrifuged at 1700× g for 5min and absorbance of the hemoglobin supernatant was measured at 540nm using a spectrophotometer (Synergy™ HT, Bio-Tek®, Winooski, VT, USA). Percent hemolysis was calculated using the following formula [18,20]:
(2)
Hemolysis
( % ) =
ABS
sam
−
ABS neg
ABS pos
−
ABS neg
× 100
where, ABSsam, ABSneg, and ABSpos are the absorbance values of the test sample, negative control (1.5mL PBS), and positive control (distilled water), respectively. The positive control was produced by adding packed RBCs to distilled water at a mixing ratio of 1:6.5, respectively.

The deformability of RBCs was measured using a commercial ektacytometer (Rheoscan-AnD300, RheoMeditech, Seoul, Korea) at 37°C. After 1-, 2-, and 3-h incubation, the GQD-exposed RBC samples were centrifuged at 1700× g for 5min and washed twice with PBS; next, 6μL of the packed RBCs was mixed with a polyvinylpyrrolidone solution and 0.5mL of the RBC suspension was loaded into a disposable chip (K-02, RheoMeditech). A piston pump was used to flow the RBC suspension into the slit micro channel of the chip. A laser beam, which was passed through the test sample, formed diffraction images of the RBCs on a semitransparent screen. The scattered images were captured using a charge-coupled device video camera at various shear stresses, as flow rate varied with time. The elongation index (EI) was calculated from the captured RBC images [28].

GQD-exposed RBC samples were centrifuged at 1700× g for 5min and washed twice after 1, 2, and 3h. The packed RBCs were added to the plasma and the final RBC suspension had a 45% hematocrit. Ten microliters of RBC suspension was injected into the inlet port of the microchip (C-01, RheoMeditech) to measure RBC aggregation using an ektacytometer. The RBC aggregation index (AI) was calculated from the transmitted light intensity curve [29].

After 3-h incubation, RBCs were exposed to different concentrations of BGQDs, washed twice with PBS, and then isolated by centrifugation (1700× g for 5min). The packed RBCs were then diluted with PBS at a mixing ratio of 1:10. The diluted RBC suspension was dropped onto a glass coverslip and fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde (Sigma–Aldrich) for 30min. The RBC suspension was dehydrated by gradual exposure to increasing concentrations (25%, 50%, 70%, 90%, and 100%) of ethanol for 10min each. The dried RBC pellet on the glass coverslip was then sputtered with platinum, and the appearance of RBCs was observed using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4700, Tokyo, Japan).

All data were analyzed using an independent sample t-test using SPSS version 22 software (SPSS, Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). The data were reported as the means±standard deviation (SD). The analyzed group differences with p-values<0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Fig. 1 (a) illustrates the schematic process for the preparation of BGQDs from l-glutamic acid, which was directly heated using a heating mantle at 220°C. At this temperature, l-glutamic acid melted to form a colorless liquid, which changed to pale yellow, and then brown after a certain time, indicating the carbonization of l-glutamic acid. Next, distilled water was added to the brown liquid to dissolve and disperse the BGQDs with vigorous stirring to avoid aggregation. According to TGA data, l-glutamic acid started to melt at 200°C, completely at 220°C after a certain time, and formed BGQDs with carbonization (Fig. S1). The morphology of the BGQDs was determined by FE-SEM and showed a relatively identical size distribution of approximately 15–25nm at 100μg/mL in aqueous solution (Fig. 1(b)). In addition, hydrodynamic diameter of BGQDs by DLS also supported FESEM data with average 25±3nm (Fig. 1(b), inset). AFM images of uniform dispersion of BGQDs revealed that the height of this nanomaterial was 2–4nm, indicating that the BGQDs contained several graphene sheets (Fig. 1(c)). We also measured morphology of BGQDs using a high resolution TEM. According to TEM image, BGQDs are monodisperse with around 20nm size in diameter (Fig. 2 (a)). The BGQDs lattice distance and corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern revealed that BGQDs are almost defect-free graphene single crystals and their lattice distance is accurately measured to be 0.234nm, which is comparable to that of graphite (Fig. 1) [21].

The blue color emission is due to the synergistic effects of the C–C core emission (π–π electron transition of C–C) and surface/molecules states [30,31]. This action remarkably influenced the band gap (energy levels) and surface states of BGQDs, which enhanced the blue color emission. Analysis of the BGQDs using an energy-dispersive EDS attached to an FE-SEM showed a value of 8.11% nitrogen, indicating the presence of numerous amine groups in the BGQDs. Furthermore, oxygen and carbon contents of approximately 40.84% and 51.05%, respectively, were generated by oxidation and hydrolysis, which occurred in aqueous medium in the presence of l-glutamic acid (Fig. 3(c) and (d)).

The excitation dependent emission profiles are presented in Fig. 4 (a). When the BGQDs were excited at 330–370nm, the emission peaks became stronger and gradually redshifted. In contrast, when excited at 390–510nm, the emission spectrum was shifted to longer wavelengths. Excitation-dependent fluorescent materials can be used in multicolor imaging applications [32]. We observed concentration-dependent stability of BGQDs, as the fluorescence intensity increased with increasing BGQD concentration (Fig. 4(b)). Furthermore, we determined the BGQD stability in solutions of different pH in aqueous solutions at 500μg/mL. The results revealed that at pH 2, the BGQDs showed a stronger fluorescence intensity than at other pH values (pH 3–10) because of the excess NH2 groups in this nanomaterial (Fig. 4(c)). We also investigated the stability of BGQDs using PL spectroscopy after 4-day storage in aqueous solution at 1.5mg/mL and pH 7. The result revealed no significant decrease in fluorescence intensity for up to 4 days, indicating that the BGQDs are stable in aqueous solution (Fig. 4(d)).

In vitro cellular uptake analysis showed that the cytoplasm of the KB cells emitted green-colored fluorescence, which is characteristic of BGQDs, indicating that the BGQDs were successfully taken up by the cells (Fig. 5 (a) and (b)). We also detected no toxicity on both KB and MDCK cells at 1000μg/mL (Fig. 6 (a) and (b)). Moreover, the cytotoxic effects of PBS and BGQDs on KB cells were further verified using AM for live cell staining and propidium iodide for co-staining. In the control and BGQD-treated group, all cells exhibited green fluorescence, indicating that BGQDs were not toxic to the cells at 1000μg/mL (Fig. 6(c)).

For fluorescence bioimaging, a region of interest was selected to evaluate the fluorescence of BGQDs (Fig. 7 ). After intravenous injection of 100μL of 5mg/kg of BGQDs into mice, BGQD fluorescence in the mice body gradually increased. Gradual increases and decreases in fluorescence intensity were observed in the tumor region for 24h, indicating tumor-selective accumulation via enhanced retention permeability effects. The highest intensities of BGQDs were observed at 6h and then gradually decreased because of rapid clearance from the body. This may be because of the small size of the BGQD nanoparticles. Our results demonstrate that the synthesized BGQDs have tumor-homing properties or enhanced retention permeability effects, and are safely eliminated through organs.

We investigated the hemolytic activity of RBCs exposed to different concentrations of BGQDs for 2h. Fig. 8 shows the amount of red hemoglobin eluted from RBCs following exposure to BGQDs. The percent hemolysis values of RBCs were 0.9%, 0.98%, and 53.1% at 750, 1000, and 1250μg/mL of BGQDs, respectively. Schubert and Müller-Goymann [33] suggested that a toxicological test should be considered within 5% hemolysis for nanomaterial safety. Therefore, the safe toxicity range for BGQDs in this study was considered as 1000μg/mL to investigate the hemorheological properties of RBCs, such as deformability and aggregation, which are described in the following two sections.

Fig. 9 presents the scattering images and elongation indices of RBCs exposed to BGQDs at various concentrations and incubation times. The diffraction pattern was curve-fitted to an ellipse using a non-linear curve-fitting algorithm and then the major (L) and minor (W) axes of the ellipse were calculated to determine the elongation index (EI). The diffraction pattern was increasingly spherical at increasing BGQD concentrations, indicating reduced deformability (Fig. 9(b)). Fig. 9(c) shows that the EI values of RBCs exposed to BGQDs for 3h were insignificant up to 750μg/mL, but greatly decreased at 1000μg/mL. The EI values for each incubation time for BGQDs at 750μg/mL were insignificant compared to the values of the control sample, whereas the EI value of RBCs exposed to 750μg/mL BGQDs at 1h was significantly decreased to 0.075, which was 76.3% lower than that of the control sample (0.317).

Fig. 10 (a) illustrates a comparison of aggregation index (AI) values of RBCs exposed to different concentrations of BGQDs for 3h. The AI values of RBCs exposed to 500μg/mL or greater BGQD concentrations were significantly decreased. The AI values of RBCs exposed to 500, 750, and 1000μg/mL were 20.9, 22.8, and 11.9, respectively, which were 41%, 36%, and 66% lower than that of the control sample (35.5). The graph in Fig. 10(b) shows that the AI values of RBCs exposed to 750 and 1000μg/mL BGQD significantly decreased with increasing incubation time, except those of RBCs exposed to 750μg/mL BGQD for 1h. The EI value of RBCs exposed to 1000μg/mL for 1h rapidly decreased to 22.8, which was 36% less than that of the control sample (35.5). RBCs exposed to 1000μg/mL BGQDs for 1h showed a significantly rapid reduction in DI and AI values. The control sample showed no significant change in the AI value at each time point. When RBCs were exposed to 750 and 1000μg/mL BGQDs for 3h, the AI values of the RBCs were 22.8 and 11.9, respectively, which were 32% and 64% less than that of the control sample (33.5).

Fig. 11 shows SEM images of RBCs exposed to various concentrations of BGQDs for 3h. The control sample exhibited a typical biconcave shape, whereas the sample exposed to 500μg/mL of BGQDs appeared as echinocytes. Some RBCs exposed to 750μg/mL of BGQDs were joined to other RBCs. RBCs exposed to 1000μg/mL of BGQDs showed cell–cell fusion because of cell membrane damage. Although severe hemolysis was not observed at 1000μg/mL, hemorheological properties of RBCs such as deformability and aggregation rapidly changed and were closely related to morphological modifications due to cell membrane impairment.

Liu and Kim [34] prepared GQDs from carbon onions and nitric acid in three steps. Three days are required to synthesize and purify the GQDs. This process is time-consuming compare than our method. We rapidly synthesized BGQDs from an amino acid, l-glutamic acid, using a very simple procedure. The materials were commonly available, inexpensive, and nontoxic and contained nitrogen groups for easy surface modifications of the GQDs for various applications. Additionally, the synthesis of BGQDs involved one step, and a reaction time of approximately 1h. In our previous study, nearly 24h was required for synthesis and 48h for purification [6]. We also used a simple purification method involving centrifugation for 30min, which is very simple.

Importantly, GQDs have tunable photoluminescence properties, which can be tailored to emit a wide range of wavelengths from 400–600nm (Fig. 4(a)). This GQDs emitted light in the near infrared spectrum (approximately 600nm), which is suitable for biomedical applications. Sk et al. [35] reported that the PL of GQDs can be tuned by size, edge configuration, shape, attached chemical functionalities, heteroatom doping, and defects. These tunable properties can penetrate deep tumor tissues in mice. We also achieved approximately 24% quantum yield (Table 1), which is much better than GQDs produced from citric acid [8]. We hypothesized that GQDs produced from l-glutamic acid contain a large amount of nitrogen, creating numerous defect sites; this causes PL intensity to be higher than that of GQDs produced from citric acid. There are no nitrogen groups in GQDs produced from citric acid [35].

Wu et al. reported GQDs produced from L-glutamic acid with multicolor and high quantum yield. However, their fluorescence images under UV light was not as strong as our synthesized materials [21]. A high quantum yield of GQDs is very important for various applications. This is defined by ratio of the number of photons emitted over the number of UV photons absorbed. Because of their high quantum yield, the sensitivity of fluorescence materials showed enhanced efficacy for various purposes [36]. The fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized BGQDs was approximately 3-fold higher than that of GQDs formulated from citric acid [8]. This may be because of the heteroatom dopants [35]. Our BGQDs showed bright fluorescence images in cells compared to our previous methods using carbon fibers [6]. Our in vivo data also supported that the PL of BGQDs was found in different organs of mice and BGQDs were quickly eliminated from the mice body because of the small size of the nanoparticles [37]. This indicates that BGQDs can be used for therapeutic purposes.

We also investigated the toxicity of BGQDs in red blood cells. Our results revealed no hemolytic activity at 1000μg/mL. Some studies showed that RBCs incubated with graphene oxide or nitrogen-doped GQDs underwent 10% greater hemolysis at 25μg/mL. The differences in toxicity in RBCs between our results and those reported previously may be attributed to the synthesis methods, particle size, surface chemistry, and chemical doping, resulting in different toxicity levels and uptake mechanisms by cells [38]. Although the percent hemolysis was 0.98% at 1000μg/mL of BGQD, we observed severe deterioration of RBC deformability and aggregation. Hemolysis is proportional to the extent of membrane defects. Thus, RBC deformability and aggregation should be considered when evaluating RBC interactions with various nanomaterials, even if the rate of hemolysis is low. We hypothesize that 750μg/mL of BGQDs may have better biocompatibility than other nanomaterials. For instance, silica nanoparticles and silica nanowires at 12.5μg/mL were found to be less hemolytic [16].

BGQDs were successfully prepared using a pyrolysis process and emitted blue luminescence when irradiated with 365nm UV lamp with 24% quantum yield. According to cellular uptake analysis, BGQDs were taken up by epithelial cancer cells and accumulated mainly in the cytoplasm. Cell cytotoxicity was evaluated using an MTT assay and a high dose (1mg/mL) of BGQDs was not found to be cytotoxic to normal cells and cancer cells, as confirmed by the high cell viability. This study also investigated the hemolytic activities and morphologies of RBCs after treatment with high quantum yield BGQDs. No significant changes in hemolytic activities of RBCs were observed at 1mg/mL BGQDs, indicating that BGQDs are not toxic to RBCs at 1mg/mL. Therefore, our prepared BGQDs with strong PL and good biocompatibility are promising for biological and medical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Technology Innovation Program [10054687] and funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MI, Korea). This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grant funded by the Korean Government [NRF-2015R1D1A1A01060863, NRF-2016H1D5A1910188 and NRF-2015R1D1A1A09060567].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2017.08.019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are Supplementary data to this article:
Fig. S1
Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) analysis of l-glutamic acid and BGQDs.
